fields.title: Project Charter Fundamentals
fields.description: Learn the fundamentals of project charters and their importance in project management.
fields.key_concepts:
• Understanding project charters
• Project authorization
• Stakeholder identification
• Scope definition
• Project objectives
fields.best_practices:
• Keep it concise
• Get stakeholder approval
• Define clear objectives
• Include success criteria
• Document assumptions

fields.title: Project Planning Basics
fields.description: Master the basics of project planning and organization.
fields.key_concepts:
• Project planning fundamentals
• Work breakdown structure
• Resource allocation
• Timeline planning
• Risk identification
fields.best_practices:
• Define clear milestones
• Include all stakeholders
• Consider constraints
• Plan for risks
• Document assumptions

fields.title: Stakeholder Management
fields.description: Understanding stakeholder management and communication strategies.
fields.key_concepts:
• Stakeholder identification
• Power/Interest matrix
• Communication planning
• Engagement strategies
• Influence mapping
fields.best_practices:
• Regular updates
• Clear communication channels
• Stakeholder mapping
• Engagement tracking
• Feedback loops

fields.text: What is the primary purpose of a project charter?
fields.explanation: A project charter formally authorizes the project and provides the project manager with the authority to apply organizational resources.

fields.text: Who typically approves the project charter?
fields.explanation: The project sponsor or senior management typically approves the project charter.

fields.text: What should be included in project success criteria?
fields.explanation: Success criteria should be specific, measurable, and aligned with business objectives.

fields.text: When should a project charter be created?
fields.explanation: A project charter should be created at the beginning of the project, before detailed planning begins.

fields.text: What role does the project manager play in creating the charter?
fields.explanation: The project manager typically assists in creating the charter but is not the one who authorizes it.

fields.text: What is a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)?
fields.explanation: A WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team.

fields.text: How should project risks be prioritized?
fields.explanation: Risks should be prioritized based on their probability of occurrence and potential impact on the project.

fields.text: What is resource leveling?
fields.explanation: Resource leveling is a technique that adjusts the project schedule to optimize the allocation of resources.

fields.text: What is the critical path in project planning?
fields.explanation: The critical path is the sequence of activities that determines the shortest possible project duration.

fields.text: What is the purpose of a project baseline?
fields.explanation: A project baseline provides the approved starting point for measuring project performance and tracking changes.

fields.text: To formally authorize the project and assign resources
fields.explanation: Correct! The project charter is the formal document that authorizes the project and assigns resources.

fields.text: To create a detailed project schedule
fields.explanation: Incorrect. Project schedules are created during project planning, not in the charter.

fields.text: Project Sponsor
fields.explanation: Correct! The project sponsor typically approves the project charter.

fields.text: Project Manager
fields.explanation: Incorrect. The project manager helps create the charter but doesn't approve it.

fields.text: Measurable outcomes aligned with business objectives
fields.explanation: Correct! Success criteria must be measurable and aligned with business goals.

fields.text: Technical specifications
fields.explanation: Incorrect. Technical specifications are part of project planning, not success criteria.

fields.text: At project initiation
fields.explanation: Correct! The charter should be created during project initiation.

fields.text: During project execution
fields.explanation: Incorrect. The charter needs to be in place before execution begins.

fields.text: Contributes to creation but doesn't authorize
fields.explanation: Correct! The PM helps create the charter but doesn't authorize it.

fields.text: Has sole authority to approve
fields.explanation: Incorrect. The project sponsor, not the PM, approves the charter.

fields.text: A hierarchical breakdown of project work
fields.explanation: Correct! A WBS breaks down the total project scope into manageable components.

fields.text: A project timeline
fields.explanation: Incorrect. While related, a WBS is about scope breakdown, not time scheduling.

fields.text: Based on probability and impact
fields.explanation: Correct! Risk priority is determined by both likelihood and potential impact.

fields.text: Only by their potential cost
fields.explanation: Incorrect. Cost is just one factor; probability must also be considered.

fields.text: A technique to optimize resource allocation
fields.explanation: Correct! Resource leveling helps balance resource usage across the project.

fields.text: Hiring more resources
fields.explanation: Incorrect. Resource leveling works with existing resources, not adding new ones.

fields.text: The sequence determining project duration
fields.explanation: Correct! The critical path shows the shortest possible completion time.

fields.text: The most expensive project tasks
fields.explanation: Incorrect. The critical path is about time dependencies, not cost.

fields.text: To measure performance and track changes
fields.explanation: Correct! The baseline is the reference point for measuring project progress.

fields.text: To list project requirements
fields.explanation: Incorrect. Requirements are documented separately from the baseline.
